{{short description|Concept in psychology}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=September 2020}}
{{emotion}}
The '''self-regulation of emotion''' or '''emotion regulation''' is the ability to respond to the ongoing demands of [[experience]] with the range of [[emotion]]s in a manner that is socially tolerable and sufficiently flexible to permit spontaneous reactions as well as the ability to delay spontaneous reactions as needed.<ref>{{Cite journal |jstor = 1166139|title = The Development of Emotion Regulation and Dysregulation: A Clinical Perspective|journal = Monographs of the Society for Research in Child Development|volume = 59|issue = 2/3|pages = 73–100|last1 = Cole|first1 = Pamela M.|last2 = Michel|first2 = Margaret K.|last3 = Teti|first3 = Laureen O'Donnell|year = 1994|doi = 10.1111/j.1540-5834.1994.tb01278.x|pmid = 7984169}}</ref> It can also be defined as [[extrinsic]] and [[intrinsic]] processes responsible for monitoring, evaluating, and modifying emotional reactions.<ref name="Thompson, 1994">{{Cite journal | doi=10.1111/j.1540-5834.1994.tb01276.x |title = Emotion Regulation: A Theme in Search of Definition|journal = Monographs of the Society for Research in Child Development|volume = 59|issue = 2–3|pages = 25–52|year = 1994|last1 = Thompson|first1 = Ross A.|pmid=7984164|url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/15215405}}</ref> The [[Self-regulation theory|self-regulation]] of emotion belongs to the broader set of emotion regulation processes, which includes both the regulation of one's own feelings and the [[interpersonal emotion regulation|regulation of other people's feelings]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Niven | first1 = K. | last2 = Totterdell | first2 = P. | last3 = Holman | first3 = D. | year = 2009 | title = A classification of controlled interpersonal affect regulation strategies | journal = Emotion | volume = 9 | issue = 4| pages = 498–509 | doi=10.1037/a0015962| pmid = 19653772 | s2cid = 565189 | url = https://www.research.manchester.ac.uk/portal/en/publications/a-classification-of-controlled-interpersonal-affect-regulation-strategies(e85181df-cb09-4c03-ae83-21e1f697d215).html }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Burman| first1 = J. T. | last2 = Green | first2 = C. D. | last3 = Shanker | first3 = S. | year = 2015 | title = On the Meanings of Self-Regulation: Digital Humanities in Service of Conceptual Clarity | journal = Child Development | volume = 86 | issue = 5 | pages = 1507–1521 | doi=10.1111/cdev.12395 | pmid=26234744| s2cid = 31507777 | url = https://pure.rug.nl/ws/files/35066226/2015_Burman_Green_Shanker_On_the_meanings_of_self_reg.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1080/08870449808407425| title=Self-regulation, health, and behavior: A perceptual-cognitive approach| journal=Psychology & Health| volume=13| issue=4| pages=717–733| year=1998| last1=Leventhal| first1=Howard| last2=Leventhal| first2=Elaine A.| last3=Contrada| first3=Richard J.}}</ref>

Emotion regulation is a complex process that involves initiating, inhibiting, or modulating one's state or [[Behavior#Biology|behavior]] in a given situation — for example, the subjective experience (feelings), cognitive responses (thoughts), emotion-related [[Signs and symptoms|physiological responses]] (for example heart rate or hormonal activity), and emotion-related behavior (bodily actions or expressions). Functionally, emotion regulation can also refer to processes such as the tendency to focus one's attention to a task and the ability to suppress inappropriate behavior under instruction. Emotion regulation is a highly significant function in human life.<ref>{{Citation |last1=Cicchetti |first1=Dante |title=Contributions from the study of high-risk populations to understanding the development of emotion regulation |date=1991 |work=The Development of Emotion Regulation and Dysregulation |pages=15–48 |editor-last=Garber |editor-first=Judy |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/abs/development-of-emotion-regulation-and-dysregulation/contributions-from-the-study-of-highrisk-populations-to-understanding-the-development-of-emotion-regulation/678481FCF6F6CE622327946E4474742C |access-date=2024-11-21 |series=Cambridge Studies in Social and Emotional Development |place=Cambridge |publisher=Cambridge University Press |doi=10.1017/cbo9780511663963.003 |isbn=978-0-521-36406-5 |last2=Ganiban |first2=Jody |last3=Barnett |first3=Douglas |editor2-last=Dodge |editor2-first=Kenneth A.|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Every day, people are continually exposed to a wide variety of potentially arousing [[Stimulus (psychology)|stimuli]]. Inappropriate, extreme or unchecked emotional reactions to such stimuli could impede functional fit within society; therefore, people must engage in some form of emotion regulation almost all of the time.<ref>{{Cite journal | doi=10.1080/02699930802619031 |title = The psychology of emotion regulation: An integrative review|journal = Cognition & Emotion|volume = 23|issue=1|pages = 4–41|year = 2009|last1 = Koole|first1 = Sander L.|s2cid = 145107160|url = https://research.vu.nl/ws/files/2599118/Koole%20Cognition%20and%20Emotion%2023(1)%202009%20u.pdf}}</ref> Generally speaking, [[emotional dysregulation|emotion dysregulation]] has been defined as difficulties in controlling the influence of emotional [[arousal]] on the organization and quality of thoughts, actions, and interactions.<ref>{{Cite web|title=What Is Emotional Dysregulation?|url=https://www.webmd.com/mental-health/what-is-emotional-dysregulation|access-date=2021-07-07|website=WebMD|language=en}}</ref> Individuals who are emotionally dysregulated exhibit patterns of responding in which there is a mismatch between their goals, responses, and/or modes of expression, and the demands of the [[social environment]].<ref name="ReferenceA">{{cite journal | last1 = Zeman | first1 = J. | last2 = Cassano | first2 = M. | last3 = Perry-Parrish | first3 = C. | last4 = Stegall | first4 = S. | year = 2006 | title = Emotion regulation in children and adolescents | journal = Journal of Developmental and Behavioral Pediatrics | volume = 27 | issue = 2| pages = 155–168 | doi=10.1097/00004703-200604000-00014| pmid = 16682883 | s2cid = 8662305 | url = https://repositorio.unal.edu.co/handle/unal/81301 | url-access = subscription }}</ref> For example, there is a significant association between emotion dysregulation and symptoms of [[Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder|ADHD]], [[Depression (mood)|depression]], [[anxiety]], [[Eating disorder|eating pathology]], and [[substance abuse]].<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal | doi=10.1016/j.cpr.2009.11.004 |pmid = 20015584|title = Emotion-regulation strategies across psychopathology: A meta-analytic review|journal = Clinical Psychology Review|volume = 30|issue = 2|pages = 217–237|year = 2010|last1 = Aldao|first1 = Amelia|last2 = Nolen-Hoeksema|first2 = Susan|last3 = Schweizer|first3 = Susanne| s2cid=14248740 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Aldao | first1 = A. | last2 = Nolen-Hoeksema | first2 = S. | year = 2010 | title = Specificity of cognitive emotion regulation strategies: a transdiagnostic examination | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 48 | issue = 10| pages = 974–983 | doi=10.1016/j.brat.2010.06.002| pmid = 20591413 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Soler-Gutiérrez |first1=Ana-María |last2=Pérez-González |first2=Juan-Carlos |last3=Mayas |first3=Julia |date=2023-01-06 |title=Evidence of emotion dysregulation as a core symptom of adult ADHD: A systematic review |journal=PLOS ONE |language=en |volume=18 |issue=1 |article-number=e0280131 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0280131 |doi-access=free |issn=1932-6203 |pmc=9821724 |pmid=36608036 |bibcode=2023PLoSO..1880131S }}</ref> Individuals diagnosed with [[mood disorder]]s and [[anxiety disorder]]s also experience dysfunction in the automatic regulation of emotions, further impacting their emotion regulation abilities.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dalton |first1=Stefan Daniel Paul |last2=Cooper |first2=Holly |last3=Jennings |first3=Ben |last4=Cheeta |first4=Survjit |date=2025-07-01 |title=The empirical status of implicit emotion regulation in mood and anxiety disorders: A meta-analytic review |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0165032725004665 |journal=Journal of Affective Disorders |volume=380 |pages=256–269 |doi=10.1016/j.jad.2025.03.118 |pmid=40122263 |issn=0165-0327|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Higher levels of emotion regulation are likely to be related to both high levels of social [[Communicative competence|competence]] and the expression of socially appropriate emotions.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Fabes | first1 = R. A. | last2 = Eisenberg | first2 = N. | last3 = Jones | first3 = S. | last4 = Smith | first4 = M. | last5 = Guthrie | first5 = I. | last6 = Poulin | first6 = R. | last7 = Shepard | first7 = S. | last8 = Friedman | first8 = J. | year = 1999 | title = Regulation, emotionality, and pre-schoolers' socially competent peer interactions | journal = Child Development | volume = 70 | issue = 2| pages = 432–442 | doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00031| pmid = 10218264 }}</ref><ref>Pulkkinen, L. (1982). Self-control and continuity from childhood to late adolescence. In P. B. Bakes & O. Brim Jr. (Eds.), Life-span development and behavior (Vol. 4, pp. 63-105). New York: Academic Press.</ref>

==Theory==

===Process model===
The process model of emotion regulation is based upon the modal model of emotion. The modal model of emotion suggests that the emotion generation process occurs in a particular sequence over time. This sequence occurs as follows:
# Situation: the sequence begins with a situation (real or imagined) that is emotionally relevant.
# [[Attention]]: attention is directed towards the emotional situation.
# [[Cognitive appraisal|Appraisal]]: the emotional situation is evaluated and interpreted.
# Response: an emotional response is generated, giving rise to loosely coordinated changes in experiential, behavioral, and physiological response systems.
Because an emotional response (4.) can cause changes to a situation (1.), this model involves a feedback loop from (4.) Response to (1.) Situation. This feedback loop suggests that the emotion generation process can occur recursively, is ongoing, and dynamic.<ref name=":0">Gross, J. J. & Thompson, R. A. (2007). Emotion regulation: Conceptual foundations. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation'' (pp. 3-24). New York: Guilford Press.</ref>

The process model contends that each of these four points in the emotion generation process can be subjected to regulation. From this conceptualization, the process model posits five different families of emotion regulation that correspond to the regulation of a particular point in the emotion generation process. They occur in the following order:<ref name=":3">{{cite journal | last1 = Gross | first1 = J. J. | year = 1998 | title = The emerging field of emotion regulation: An integrative review | journal = Review of General Psychology | volume = 2 | issue = 3| pages = 271–299 | doi=10.1037/1089-2680.2.3.271| citeseerx = 10.1.1.476.7042 | s2cid = 6236938 }}</ref>
# Situation selection
# Situation modification
# Attentional deployment
# Cognitive change
# Response modulation
The process model also divides these emotion regulation strategies into two categories: antecedent-focused and response-focused. Antecedent-focused strategies (i.e., situation selection, situation modification, attentional deployment, and cognitive change) occur before an emotional response is fully generated. Response-focused strategies (i.e., response modulation) occur after an emotional response is fully generated.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal | last1 = Gross | first1 = J. J. | year = 1998 | title = Antecedent- and response-focused emotion regulation: Divergent consequences for experience, expression, and physiology | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 74 | issue = 1 | pages = 224–237 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.74.1.224| pmid = 9457784 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.688.783 | s2cid = 3031566 }}</ref>

==Strategies==

===Situation selection===
Situation selection is an emotional regulation strategy that involves choosing to avoid or approach a future emotional situation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Thuillard |first1=Simon |last2=Dan-Glauser |first2=Elise S. |date=March 2021 |title=Efficiency of Illusory Choice Used as a Variant of Situation Selection for Regulating Emotions: Reduction of Positive Experience But Preservation of Physiological Downregulation |journal=Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback |language=en |volume=46 |issue=1 |pages=115–132 |doi=10.1007/s10484-020-09484-x |issn=1090-0586 |pmc=7878267 |pmid=32770450}}</ref> If a person selects to avoid or disengage from an emotionally relevant situation, they are decreasing the likelihood of experiencing an emotion. Alternatively, if a person selects to approach or engage with an emotionally relevant situation, they are increasing the likelihood of experiencing an emotion.<ref name=":3" />

Typical examples of situation selection may be seen interpersonally, such as when a parent removes his or her child from an emotionally unpleasant situation.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Fox | first1 = N. A. | last2 = Calkins | first2 = S. D. | year = 2003 | title = The development of self-control of emotion: Intrinsic and extrinsic influences | journal = Motivation and Emotion | volume = 27 | issue = 1| pages = 7–26 | doi = 10.1023/A:1023622324898 | s2cid = 6467799 }}</ref> Use of situation selection may also be seen in psychopathology. For example, avoidance of social situations to regulate emotions is particularly pronounced for those with [[social anxiety disorder]]<ref name=":10">{{cite journal | last1 = Wells | first1 = A. | last2 = Papageorgiou | first2 = C. | year = 1998 | title = Social phobia: Effects of external attention on anxiety, negative beliefs, and perspective taking | journal = Behavior Therapy | volume = 29 | issue = 3| pages = 357–370 | doi=10.1016/s0005-7894(98)80037-3}}</ref> and [[avoidant personality disorder]].<ref name=":9" />

Effective situation selection is not always an easy task. For instance, humans display difficulties [[Affective forecasting|predicting their emotional responses]] to future events. Therefore, they may have trouble making accurate and appropriate decisions about which emotionally relevant situations to approach or to avoid.<ref>Loewenstein, G. (2007). Affect regulation and affective forecasting. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation ''(pp.180-203). New York: Guilford Press.</ref>

===Situation modification===
Situation modification involves efforts to modify a situation so as to change its emotional impact.<ref name=":3" /> Situation modification refers specifically to altering one's external, physical environment. Altering one's "internal" environment to regulate emotion is called cognitive change.<ref name=":0" /> Younger and older adults seem to also regulate their emotions through situation modification in differing ways that reflects what type of material they consume ranging from negative, neutral, and positive. Livingstone and Isaacowitz conducted research where they observed younger and older adults as they viewed different types of material and had the choice to skip what material they saw, and it came out that older adults are better able to regulate the negative material, focusing mainly on the positive, than the younger adults who still consumed positive material, but not as much modification. This showing that as we age, we gain a better understanding of situation modification and being able to emotionally self-regulate.<ref>Livingstone, K. M., & Isaacowitz, D. M. (2015). Situation selection and modification for emotion regulation in younger and older adults. ''Social Psychological and Personality Science'', ''6''(8), 904–910. <nowiki>https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550615593148</nowiki></ref> Beyond individual situational strategies, structured psychotherapeutic approaches such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) have demonstrated effectiveness in improving emotion regulation by targeting maladaptive cognitive patterns and enhancing coping skills.<ref name="Hofmann2012">Hofmann, S.G., Asnaani, A., Vonk, I.J., Sawyer, A.T., Fang, A. (2012). The Efficacy of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: A Review of Meta-analyses. Cognitive Therapy and Research, 36(5), 427–440. doi:10.1007/s10608-012-9476-1.</ref>

Examples of situation modification may include injecting humor into a speech to elicit laughter<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Hofmann | first1 = S. G. | last2 = Gerlach | first2 = A. L. | last3 = Wender | first3 = A. | last4 = Roth | first4 = W T. | year = 1997 | title = Speech disturbances and gaze behavior during public speaking in subtypes of social phobia | journal = Journal of Anxiety Disorders | volume = 11 | issue = 6| pages = 573–585 | doi=10.1016/s0887-6185(97)00040-6| pmid = 9455720 | doi-access = free }}</ref> or extending the physical distance between oneself and another person.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Edelmann | first1 = R. J. | last2 = Iwawaki | first2 = S. | year = 1987 | title = Self-reported expression and consequences of embarrassment in the United Kingdom and Japan | journal = Psychologia | volume = 30 | issue = 4| pages = 205–216 }}</ref>

===Attentional deployment===
Attentional deployment involves directing one's attention towards or away from an emotional situation.<ref name=":3" />

====Distraction====
[[Distraction]], an example of attentional deployment, is an early selection strategy, which involves diverting one's attention away from an emotional stimulus and towards other content.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sheppes | first1 = G. | last2 = Gross | first2 = J. J. | year = 2011 | title = Is timing everything? Temporal considerations in emotion regulation | journal = Personality and Social Psychology Review | volume = 15 | issue = 4| pages = 319–331 | doi=10.1177/1088868310395778| pmid = 21233326 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.688.4292 | s2cid = 637723 }}</ref> Distraction has been shown to reduce the intensity of painful<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Seminowicz | first1 = D. A. | last2 = Davis | first2 = K. D. | year = 2007 | title = Interactions of pain intensity and cognitive load: the brain stays on task | journal = Cerebral Cortex | volume = 17 | issue = 6| pages = 1412–1422 | doi=10.1093/cercor/bhl052 | pmid=16908493| doi-access = free }}</ref> and emotional experiences,<ref name=":1">{{cite journal | last1 = Urry | first1 = H. L. | year = 2010 | title = Seeing, thinking, and feeling: emotion-regulating effects of gaze-directed cognitive reappraisal | journal = Emotion | volume = 10 | issue = 1| pages = 125–135 | doi=10.1037/a0017434| pmid = 20141309 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.514.4324 }}</ref> to decrease facial responding and neural activation in the amygdala associated with emotion,<ref name=":1" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Kanske|first1=Philipp|last2=Heissler|first2=Janine|last3=Schönfelder|first3=Sandra|last4=Bongers|first4=André|last5=Wessa|first5=Michèle|date=2011-06-01|title=How to Regulate Emotion? Neural Networks for Reappraisal and Distraction|journal=Cerebral Cortex|volume=21|issue=6|pages=1379–1388|doi=10.1093/cercor/bhq216|pmid=21041200|issn=1047-3211|doi-access=free}}</ref> as well as to alleviate emotional distress.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nolen-Hoeksema | first1 = S. | last2 = Morrow | first2 = J. | year = 1993 | title = Effects of rumination and distraction on naturally occurring depressed mood | journal = Cognition and Emotion | volume = 7 | issue = 6| pages = 561–570 | doi=10.1080/02699939308409206}}</ref> As opposed to [[#Reappraisal|reappraisal]], individuals show a relative preference to engage in distraction when facing stimuli of high negative emotional intensity. This is because distraction easily filters out high-intensity emotional content, which would otherwise be relatively difficult to appraise and process.<ref name=":5">{{cite journal | last1 = Sheppes | first1 = G. | last2 = Scheibe | first2 = S. | last3 = Suri | first3 = G. | last4 = Gross | first4 = J. J. | s2cid = 5283764 | year = 2011 | title = Emotion-regulation choice | journal = Psychological Science | volume = 22 | issue = 11| pages = 1391–1396 | doi=10.1177/0956797611418350| pmid = 21960251 | url = https://research.rug.nl/en/publications/32e89145-634f-45f6-b0dd-d015d8048ea2 }}</ref>

====Rumination====
[[Rumination (psychology)|Rumination]], an example of attentional deployment,<ref name=":9" /> is defined as the passive and repetitive focusing of one's attention on one's symptoms of distress and the causes and consequences of these symptoms. Rumination is generally considered a maladaptive emotion regulation strategy, as it tends to exacerbate emotional distress. It has also been implicated in a host of disorders including [[Major depressive disorder|major depression]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Nolen-Hoeksema | first1 = S. | last2 = Wisco | first2 = B. E. | last3 = Lyubomirsky | first3 = S. | year = 2008 | title = Rethinking rumination | journal = Perspectives on Psychological Science | volume = 3 | issue = 5| pages = 400–424 | doi=10.1111/j.1745-6924.2008.00088.x| pmid = 26158958 | s2cid = 6415609 | url = https://escholarship.org/uc/item/8544f7w2 }}</ref>

====Worry====
[[Worry]], an example of attentional deployment,<ref name=":9" /> involves directing attention to thoughts and images concerned with potentially negative events in the future.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Borkovec | first1 = T. D. | last2 = Robinson | first2 = E. | last3 = Pruzinsky | first3 = T. | last4 = DePree | first4 = J. A. | year = 1983 | title = Preliminary exploration of worry: Some characteristics and processes | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 21 | issue = 1| pages = 9–16 | doi=10.1016/0005-7967(83)90121-3| pmid = 6830571 }}</ref> By focusing on these events, worrying serves to aid in the down-regulation of intense negative emotion and physiological activity.<ref name=":9" /> While worry may sometimes involve problem solving, incessant worry is generally considered maladaptive, being a common feature of [[anxiety disorders]], particularly [[generalized anxiety disorder]].<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Borkovec | first1 = T. D. | last2 = Inz | first2 = J. | year = 1990 | title = The nature of worry in generalized anxiety disorder: A predominance of thought activity | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 28 | issue = 2| pages = 153–158 | doi=10.1016/0005-7967(90)90027-g| pmid = 2183759 }}</ref>

====Thought suppression====
{{Main|Thought suppression}}
Thought suppression, an example of attentional deployment, involves efforts to redirect one's attention from specific thoughts and mental images to other content so as to modify one's emotional state.<ref name=":9">Campbell-Sills, L. & Barlow, D. H. (2007). Incorporating emotion regulation into conceptualizations and treatments of anxiety and mood disorders. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation'' (pp. 542–559). New York: Guilford Press.</ref> Although thought suppression may provide temporary relief from undesirable thoughts, it may ironically end up spurring the production of even more unwanted thoughts.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Wegner | first1 = D. M. | last2 = Zanakos | first2 = S. | year = 1994 | title = Chronic thought suppression | journal = Journal of Personality | volume = 62 | issue = 4| pages = 615–640 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-6494.1994.tb00311.x| pmid = 7861307 }}</ref> This strategy is generally considered maladaptive, being most associated with [[obsessive-compulsive disorder]].<ref name=":9" />

===Cognitive change===
Cognitive change involves changing how one appraises a situation so as to alter its emotional meaning.<ref name=":3" />

====Reappraisal====
Reappraisal, an example of cognitive change, is a late selection strategy, which involves a change of the meaning of an event that alters its emotional impact.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Davis| first1 = J. I. | last2 = Gross| first2 = J. J. | last3 = Ochsner | first3 = K. N. | year = 2011 | title =Psychological distance and emotional experience: What you see is what you get | journal = Emotion | volume = 11 | issue = 2 | pages = 438–444 | doi=10.1037/a0021783| pmid = 21500912 }}</ref> It encompasses different substrategies, such as positive reappraisal (creating and focusing on a positive aspect of the stimulus),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Moster| first1 =J. S. | last2 = Hartwig| first2 = R. | last3 = Moran| first3 = T. P. | last4 = Jendrusina | first4 = A. A. | last5 = Kross | first5 = E. | year = 2014 | title = Neural markers of positive reappraisal and their associations with trait reappraisal and worry | journal = Journal of Abnormal Psychology | volume = 123 | issue = 1| pages = 91–105 | doi=10.1037/a0035817| pmid =24661162 }}</ref> decentering (reinterpreting an event by broadening one's perspective to see "the bigger picture"),<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Schartau | first1 = P. E. | last2 = Dalgleish | first2 = T. | last3 = Dunn | first3 = B. D. | year = 2009 | title = Seeing the bigger picture: training in perspective broadening reduces self-reported affect and psychophysiological response to distressing films and autobiographical memories | journal = Journal of Abnormal Psychology | volume = 118 | issue = 1| pages = 15–27 | doi=10.1037/a0012906 | pmid=19222310| s2cid = 33850126 }}</ref> or fictional reappraisal (adopting or emphasizing the belief that event is not real, that it is for instance "just a movie" or "just my imagination").<ref name="makowski2019">{{cite journal |last1=Makowski |first1=D. |last2=Sperduti |first2=M. |last3=Pelletier |first3=J. |last4=Blondé |first4=P. |last5=La Corte |first5=V. |last6=Arcangeli |first6=M. |last7=Zalla |first7=T. |last8=Lemaire |first8=S. |last9=Dokic |first9=J. |last10=Nicolas |first10=S. |last11=Piolino |first11=P. |date=January 2019 |title=Phenomenal, bodily and brain correlates of fictional reappraisal as an implicit emotion regulation strategy |journal=Cognitive, Affective, & Behavioral Neuroscience |volume=19|issue=4 |pages=877–897|doi=10.3758/s13415-018-00681-0|pmid=30610654 |s2cid=58591122 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Reappraisal has been shown to effectively reduce physiological,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Jackson | first1 = D. C. | last2 = Malmstadt | first2 = J. R. | last3 = Larson | first3 = C. L. | last4 = Davidson | first4 = R. J. | year = 2000 | title = Suppression and enhancement of emotional responses to unpleasant pictures | journal = Psychophysiology | volume = 37 | issue = 4| pages = 515–522 | doi=10.1111/1469-8986.3740515| pmid = 10934910 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.668.4076 }}</ref> subjective,<ref name=":4" /> and neural<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ochsner | first1 = K. N. | last2 = Ray | first2 = R. R. | last3 = Cooper | first3 = J. C. | last4 = Robertson | first4 = E. R. | last5 = Chopra | first5 = S. | last6 = Gabrieli | first6 = J. D. E. | last7 = Gross | first7 = J. J. | year = 2004 | title = For better or for worse: Neural systems supporting the cognitive down- and up-regulation of negative emotion | journal = NeuroImage | volume = 23 | issue = 2| pages = 483–499 | doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2004.06.030| pmid = 15488398 | s2cid = 16146101 }}</ref> emotional responding. As opposed to distraction, individuals show a relative preference to engage in reappraisal when facing stimuli of low negative emotional intensity because these stimuli are relatively easy to appraise and process.<ref name=":5" />

Reappraisal is generally considered to be an adaptive emotion regulation strategy. Compared to suppression (including both thought suppression and [[expressive suppression]]), which is positively correlated with many psychological disorders,<ref name=":6" /> reappraisal can be associated with better interpersonal outcomes, and can be positively related to well-being.<ref name=":7">{{cite journal|author1=Gross, James |author2=John, Oliver |title=Individual differences in two emotion regulation processes: Implications for affect, relationships, and well-being|pmid=12916575|year=2003|volume=85|issue=2|pages=348–62|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.85.2.348|bibcode=2003JPSP...85..348G |citeseerx=10.1.1.688.115 |s2cid=20541103 }}</ref> However, some researchers argue that context is important when evaluating the adaptiveness of a strategy, suggesting that in some contexts reappraisal may be maladaptive.<ref name="Tamir, M. 2009">{{cite journal | last1 = Tamir | first1 = M | year = 2009 | title = What do people want to feel and why? Pleasure and utility in emotion regulation | journal = Current Directions in Psychological Science | volume = 18 | issue = 2| pages = 101–105 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-8721.2009.01617.x| citeseerx = 10.1.1.454.6507 | s2cid = 13845576 }}</ref> Furthermore, some research has shown reappraisal does not influence or affect physiological responses to recurrent stress.<ref name="Griffin, S. M. 2021">{{cite journal | last1 = Griffin| first1 = S.M.| last2=Howard|first2 = S.|title=Instructed reappraisal and cardiovascular habituation to recurrent stress|year=2021|journal=Psychophysiology| volume = 58| issue = 5| article-number = e13783|doi=10.1111/psyp.13783| pmid = 33538020| url = http://psyarxiv.com/h7t6n/| hdl = 10344/10521| s2cid = 231804264| hdl-access = free}}</ref>

====Distancing====
Distancing, an example of cognitive change, involves taking on an independent, third-person perspective when evaluating an emotional event.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ochsner | first1 = K. N. | last2 = Gross | first2 = J. J. | year = 2008 | title = Cognitive emotion regulation: Insights from social, cognitive, affective neuroscience | journal = Current Directions in Psychological Science | volume = 17 | issue = 2| pages = 153–158 | doi=10.1111/j.1467-8721.2008.00566.x| pmid = 25425765 | pmc = 4241349 }}</ref> Distancing has been shown to be an adaptive form of self-reflection, facilitating the emotional processing of negatively valenced stimuli,<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ayduk | first1 = O. | last2 = Kross | first2 = E. | year = 2009 | title = Asking why from a distance facilitates emotional processing: A reanalysis of Wimalaweera and Moulds (2008) | journal = Behaviour Research and Therapy | volume = 47 | issue = 1| pages = 88–92 | doi=10.1016/j.brat.2008.06.014| pmid = 19013553 }}</ref> reducing emotional and cardiovascular reactivity to negative stimuli, and increasing problem-solving behavior.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ayduk | first1 = O. | last2 = Kross | first2 = E. | year = 2010 | title = From a distance: Implications of spontaneous self-distancing for adaptive self-reflection | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 98 | issue = 5| pages = 809–829 | doi=10.1037/a0019205 | pmid=20438226 | pmc=2881638}}</ref>

====Humour====
[[Humour]], an example of cognitive change, has been shown to be an effective emotion regulation strategy. Specifically, positive, good-natured humour has been shown to effectively [[Upregulation (psychology)|up-regulate]] positive emotion and down-regulate negative emotion. On the other hand, negative, mean-spirited humour is less effective in this regard.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Samson | first1 = A. C. | last2 = Gross | first2 = J. J. | year = 2012 | title = Humour as emotion regulation: The differential consequences of negative versus positive humour | url = https://archive-ouverte.unige.ch/unige:98021| journal = Cognition and Emotion | volume = 26 | issue = 2| pages = 375–384 | doi=10.1080/02699931.2011.585069| pmid = 21756218 | s2cid = 1173305 }}</ref>

===Response modulation===
Response modulation involves attempts to directly influence experiential, behavioral, and physiological response systems.<ref name=":3" />

====Expressive suppression====
{{Main|Expressive suppression}}
Expressive suppression, an example of response modulation, involves inhibiting emotional expressions. It has been shown to effectively reduce facial expressivity, subjective feelings of positive emotion, heart rate, and [[Sympathetic nervous system|sympathetic activation]]. However, the research findings are mixed regarding whether this strategy is effective for down-regulating negative emotion.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Dan-Glauser | first1 = E. S. | last2 = Gross | first2 = J. J. | year = 2011 | title = The temporal dynamics of two response-focused forms of emotion regulation: Experiential, expressive, and autonomic consequences | journal = Psychophysiology | volume = 48 | issue = 9| pages = 1309–1322 | doi=10.1111/j.1469-8986.2011.01191.x| pmid = 21361967 | pmc = 3136552 }}</ref> Research has also shown that expressive suppression may have negative social consequences, correlating with reduced [[Social connection|personal connections]] and greater difficulties forming relationships.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Butler | first1 = E. A. | last2 = Egloff | first2 = B. | last3 = Wlhelm | first3 = F. H. | last4 = Smith | first4 = N. C. | last5 = Erickson | first5 = E. A. | last6 = Gross | first6 = J. J. | s2cid = 18315833 | year = 2003 | title = The social consequences of expressive suppression | journal = Emotion | volume = 3 | issue = 1| pages = 48–67 | doi = 10.1037/1528-3542.3.1.48 | pmid = 12899316 }}</ref>

Expressive suppression is generally considered to be a maladaptive emotion regulation strategy. Compared to reappraisal, it is positively correlated with many psychological disorders,<ref name=":6" /> associated with worse interpersonal outcomes, is negatively related to well-being,<ref name=":7" /> and requires the mobilization of a relatively substantial amount of cognitive resources.<ref name="Richards">{{cite journal|last1=Richards|first1=Jane|title=The Cognitive Consequences of Concealing Feelings|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|date=August 2004|volume=13|issue=4|pages=131–134|doi=10.1111/j.0963-7214.2004.00291.x|s2cid=146595050}}</ref> However, some researchers argue that context is important when evaluating the adaptiveness of a strategy, suggesting that in some contexts suppression may be adaptive.<ref name="Tamir, M. 2009"/>

====Drug use====
[[Drug]] use, an example of response modulation, can be used to alter emotion-associated physiological responses. For example, [[Ethanol|alcohol]] can produce [[sedative]] and [[anxiolytic]] effects<ref>Sher, K. J. & Grekin, E. R. (2007). Alcohol and affect regulation. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation'' (pp. 560–580). New York: Guilford Press.</ref> and [[beta blockers]] can affect sympathetic activation.<ref name=":0" />

====Exercise====
[[Exercise]], an example of response modulation, can be used to down-regulate the physiological and experiential effects of negative emotions.<ref name=":0" /> Regular physical activity has also been shown to reduce emotional distress and improve emotional control.<ref name=":8">{{cite journal|last1=Oaten|first1=Megan|author2=Cheng, Ken|title=Longitudinal gains in self-regulation from regular physical exercise|journal=British Journal of Health Psychology|year=2006|volume=11|issue=4|pages=717–733|doi=10.1348/135910706X96481|pmid=17032494}}</ref> Exercise has been proven to increase emotional health and regulation through hormonal regulation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Taylor |first1=C. Barr |last2=Sallis |first2=James F. |last3=Needle |first3=Richard |date=1985 |title=The Relation of Physical Activity and Exercise to Mental Health |journal=Public Health Reports |volume=100 |issue=2 |pages=195–202 |jstor=20056436 |pmid=3920718 |pmc=1424736 |issn=0033-3549}}</ref>

====Sleep====
[[Sleep]] plays a role in emotion regulation, although stress and worry can also interfere with sleep. Studies have shown that sleep, specifically [[REM sleep]], down-regulates reactivity of the [[amygdala]], a brain structure known to be involved in the processing of emotions, in response to previous emotional experiences.<ref name="walker">{{cite journal |last1=Walker |first1=Matthew P. |date=March 2009 |title=The Role of Sleep in Cognition and Emotion |url=https://walkerlab.berkeley.edu/reprints/Walker_NYAS_2009.pdf |journal=Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences |volume=1156 |issue=1 |pages=168–197 |doi=10.1111/j.1749-6632.2009.04416.x|pmid=19338508|bibcode=2009NYASA1156..168W |s2cid=313685 }}</ref> On the flip side, [[sleep deprivation]] is associated with greater emotional reactivity or overreaction to negative and stressful stimuli. This is a result of both increased amygdala activity and a disconnect between the amygdala and the [[prefrontal cortex]], which regulates the amygdala through inhibition, together resulting in an overactive emotional brain.<ref name="walker"/> Due to the subsequent lack of emotional control, sleep deprivation may be associated with depression, [[impulsivity]], and mood swings. Additionally, there is some evidence that sleep deprivation may reduce emotional reactivity to positive stimuli and events and impair [[emotion recognition]] in others.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Beattie |first1=Louise |last2=Kyle |first2=Simon D. |last3=Espie |first3=Colin A. |last4=Biello |first4=Stephany M. |date=December 2015 |title=Social interactions, emotion and sleep: A systematic review and research agenda |journal=Sleep Medicine Reviews |volume=24 |pages=83–100 |doi=10.1016/j.smrv.2014.12.005 |pmid=25697832 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

==== Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD) ====
{{Main|Borderline personality disorder}}
In its extreme form, problems with response modulation is correlated with Borderline Personality Disorder (BPD).<ref name="linkinghub.elsevier.com">{{Cite journal |last1=Rosenthal |first1=M. Zachary |last2=Gratz |first2=Kim L. |last3=Kosson |first3=David S. |last4=Cheavens |first4=Jennifer S. |last5=Lejuez |first5=C. W. |last6=Lynch |first6=Thomas R. |date=2008-01-01 |title=Borderline personality disorder and emotional responding: A review of the research literature |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S027273580700075X |journal=Clinical Psychology Review |volume=28 |issue=1 |pages=75–91 |doi=10.1016/j.cpr.2007.04.001 |pmid=17544188 |issn=0272-7358|url-access=subscription }}</ref> BPD is characterized by an enduring instability in regulating emotions, relationships with others, your self-image, and your behavior.<ref>{{Citation |title=Borderline personality disorder (BPD) |date=2018-10-08 |work=Basic Notes in Psychopharmacology |pages=77–78 |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/9781138030817/chapters/10.1201/9781315377971-12 |access-date=2024-11-21 |edition=0 |publisher=CRC Press |language=en |doi=10.1201/9781315377971-12 |isbn=978-1-315-37797-1|url-access=subscription }}</ref> This can lead to self-sabotage, risk-taking, impulsivity, and aggression.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sansone |first1=R. |last2=Sansone |first2=L. A. |date=2015-07-01 |title=BORDERLINE PERSONALITY DISORDER IN THE MEDICAL SETTING: Suggestive Behaviors, Syndromes, and Diagnoses. |journal=Innovations in Clinical Neuroscience|volume=12 |issue=7–8 |pages=39–44 |pmid=26351624 |pmc=4558791 |s2cid=22843466 }}</ref> Research has indicated that the heightened emotional response can be due to an exaggerated amygdala response and an impaired anterior cingulate cortex, which is responsible for modulating emotions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Minzenberg |first1=Michael J. |last2=Fan |first2=Jin |last3=New |first3=Antonia S. |last4=Tang |first4=Cheuk Y. |last5=Siever |first5=Larry J. |date=2007-08-15 |title=Fronto-limbic dysfunction in response to facial emotion in borderline personality disorder: An event-related fMRI study |journal=Psychiatry Research: Neuroimaging |volume=155 |issue=3 |pages=231–243 |doi=10.1016/j.pscychresns.2007.03.006 |issn=0925-4927 |pmc=2084368 |pmid=17601709}}</ref> This can lead to an intense emotional response.<ref name="linkinghub.elsevier.com"/>

==In psychotherapy==

Emotion regulation strategies are taught, and emotion regulation problems are treated, in a variety of [[counseling]] and [[psychotherapy]] approaches, including [[cognitive behavioral therapy]] (CBT), [[dialectical behavior therapy]] (DBT), [[emotion-focused therapy]] (EFT), and [[mindfulness-based cognitive therapy]] (MBCT).<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Berking |first1=Matthias |author-link1=Matthias Berking |last2=Wupperman |first2=Peggilee |last3=Reichardt |first3=Alexander |last4=Pejic |first4=Tanja |last5=Dippel |first5=Alexandra |last6=Znoj |first6=Hansjörg |date=November 2008 |title=Emotion-regulation skills as a treatment target in psychotherapy |journal=[[Behaviour Research and Therapy]] |volume=46 |issue=11 |pages=1230–1237 |doi=10.1016/j.brat.2008.08.005 |pmid=18835479}}</ref><ref>{{cite book |editor1-last=Kring |editor1-first=Ann M. |editor2-last=Sloan |editor2-first=Denise M. |date=2010 |title=Emotion Regulation and Psychopathology: A Transdiagnostic Approach to Etiology and Treatment |location=New York |publisher=[[Guilford Press]] |isbn=978-1-60623-450-1 |oclc=319318901}}</ref>

For example, a relevant [[mnemonic]] formulated in DBT is "ABC PLEASE":<ref>{{cite book |last1=Linehan |first1=Marsha M. |author-link=Marsha M. Linehan |date=2015 |title=DBT Skills Training Manual |edition=2nd |location=New York |publisher=[[Guilford Press]] |isbn=978-1-4625-1699-5 |oclc=883366057 |page=382}}</ref>
* '''A'''ccumulate positive experiences.
* '''B'''uild mastery by being active in activities that make one feel competent and effective to combat [[Learned helplessness|helplessness]].
* '''C'''ope ahead, preparing an action plan, researching, and rehearsing (with a skilled helper if necessary).
* '''P'''hysical illness treatment and prevention through checkups.
* '''L'''ow vulnerability to diseases, managed with health care professionals.
* '''E'''ating healthy.
* '''A'''voiding (non-prescribed) mood-altering drugs.
* '''S'''leep healthy.
* '''E'''xercise regularly.

==Developmental process==
{{See also|Developmental psychology|Social emotional development}}

===Infancy===
Intrinsic emotion regulation efforts during [[infancy]] are believed to be guided primarily by innate physiological response systems.<ref>Derryberr, D., & Rothbart, M. K. (2001). Early temperament and emotional development. In A. F. Kalverboer & A. Gramsbergen (Eds.), ''Handbook of Brain and Behavior in Human Development'' (pp. 967–988). Dordrecht, The Netherlands: Kluwer Academic.</ref> These systems usually manifest as an approach towards and an avoidance of pleasant or unpleasant stimuli. At three months, infants can engage in self-soothing behaviors like sucking and can reflexively respond to and signal feelings of distress.<ref name=":13">Rothbart, M., Ziaie, H., & O'Boyle, C. (1992). Self-regulation and emotion in infancy. In N. Eisenberg, & R. Fabes (Eds.), ''Emotion and Its Regulation in Early Development'' (pp. 7-23). San Francisco: Jossey-Bass/Pfeiffer.</ref> For instance, infants have been observed attempting to suppress anger or sadness by knitting their brow or compressing their lips.<ref name="Malatesta et. al.">{{cite journal|last1=Malatesta|first1=C.Z.|author2=Grigoryev, P. |author3=Lamb, C. |author4=Albin, M. |author5=Culver, C. |title=Emotional socialization and expressive development in preterm and full-term infants|journal=Child Development|year=1986|volume=57|pages=316–330|doi=10.2307/1130587|pmid=3956316|issue=2|jstor=1130587}}</ref>

Between three and six months, basic [[Motor skills|motor functioning]] and attentional mechanisms begin to play a role in emotion regulation, allowing infants to more effectively approach or avoid emotionally relevant situations.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kochanska | first1 = G. | last2 = Coy | first2 = K. C. | last3 = Murray | first3 = K. Y. | year = 2001 | title = The development of self-regulation in the first four years of life | journal = Child Development | volume = 72 | issue = 4| pages = 1091–1111 | doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00336| pmid = 11480936 | citeseerx = 10.1.1.333.4872 }}</ref> Infants may also engage in self-distraction and help-seeking behaviors for regulatory purposes.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Stifter | first1 = C. A. | last2 = Braungart | first2 = J. M. | year = 1995 | title = The regulation of negative reactivity in infancy: Function and development | journal = Developmental Psychology | volume = 31 | issue = 3| pages = 448–455 | doi=10.1037/0012-1649.31.3.448}}</ref> At one year, infants are able to navigate their surroundings more actively and respond to emotional stimuli with greater flexibility due to improved motor skills.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kopp | first1 = C | s2cid = 18870683 | year = 1982 | title = Antecedents of self-regulation: A developmental perspective | journal = Developmental Psychology | volume = 18 | issue = 2| pages = 199–214 | doi=10.1037/0012-1649.18.2.199}}</ref> They also begin to appreciate their caregivers' abilities to provide them regulatory support.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Diener | first1 = M. | last2 = Mangelsdorf | first2 = S. | last3 = McHale | first3 = J. | last4 = Frosch | first4 = C. | year = 2002 | title = Infants' behavioral strategies for emotion regulation with fathers and mothers: Associations with emotional expressions and attachment quality | journal = Infancy | volume = 3 | issue = 2| pages = 153–174 | doi=10.1207/s15327078in0302_3| pmid = 33451203 }}</ref> For instance, infants generally have difficulties regulating [[fear]].<ref name="Buss and Goldsmith">{{cite journal|last1=Buss|first1=K.A.|author2=Goldsmith, H.H.|title=Fear and anger regulation in infancy: Effects on temporal dynamics of affective expression|journal=Child Development|year=1998|volume=69|pages=359–374|pmid=9586212|issue=2|doi=10.1111/j.1467-8624.1998.tb06195.x|doi-access=free}}</ref> As a result, they often find ways to express fear in ways that attract the comfort and attention of caregivers.<ref name="Bridges & Grolnick">{{cite journal|last1=Bridges|first1=L.J.|author2=Grolnick, W.S.|title=The development of the self-regulation of emotion in infancy and early childhood|journal=Social Development|year=1995|volume=15|pages=185–211}}</ref>

Extrinsic emotion regulation efforts by caregivers, including situation selection, modification, and distraction, are particularly important for infants.<ref name=":14">Calkins, S. D., & Hill, A. (2007). Caregiver influence on emerging emotion regulation: Biological and environmental transactions in early development. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation'' (pp. 229–248). New York: Guilford Press.</ref> The emotion regulation strategies employed by caregivers to attenuate distress or to up-regulate positive affect in infants can impact the infants' emotional and behavioral development, teaching them particular strategies and methods of regulation.<ref>Sroufe, L. A. (1996). ''Emotional development: The organization of emotional life in the early years''. New York: Cambridge University Press.</ref> The type of [[Attachment styles|attachment style]] between caregiver and infant can therefore play a meaningful role in the regulatory strategies infants may learn to use.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Kochanska | first1 = G | year = 2001 | title = Emotional development in children with different attachment histories: The first three years | journal = Child Development | volume = 72 | issue = 2| pages = 474–490 | doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00291| pmid = 11333079 }}</ref>

Recent evidence supports the idea that maternal singing has a positive effect on [[affect regulation]] in infants.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Trehub | first1 = S. E. | last2 = Ghazban | first2 = N. | last3 = Corbeil | first3 = M. | year = 2015 | title = Musical affect regulation in infancy | journal = Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences | volume = 1337 | issue = 1| pages = 186–192 | doi=10.1111/nyas.12622 | pmid=25773634| bibcode = 2015NYASA1337..186T | s2cid = 39093993 }}</ref> Singing play-songs can have a visible affect-regulatory consequence of prolonged positive affect and even alleviation of distress. In addition to proven facilitation of social bonding, when combined with movement and/or rhythmic touch, maternal singing for affect regulation has possible applications for infants in the NICU and for adult caregivers with serious personality or adjustment difficulties.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Stamou |first1=Lelouda |last2=Evaggelou |first2=Faiy |last3=Stamou |first3=Vasileios |last4=Diamanti |first4=Elisavet |last5=Loewy |first5=Joanne V. |date=2020-04-24 |title=The effects of live singing on the biophysiological functions of preterm infants hospitalized in a neonatal intensive care unit in Greece: A pilot study |url=https://mmd.iammonline.com/index.php/musmed/article/view/703 |journal=Music and Medicine |language=en |volume=12 |issue=2 |pages=109–121 |doi=10.47513/mmd.v12i2.703 |issn=1943-863X|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

===Toddler-hood===
By the end of the first year, [[toddler]]s begin to adopt new strategies to decrease negative arousal. These strategies can include rocking themselves, chewing on objects, or moving away from things that upset them.<ref name="Kopp">{{Cite journal | doi=10.1037/0012-1649.25.3.343 |title = Regulation of distress and negative emotions: A developmental view|journal = Developmental Psychology|volume = 25|issue = 3|pages = 343–354|year = 1989|last1 = Kopp|first1 = Claire B.}}</ref> At two years, toddlers become more capable of actively employing emotion regulation strategies.<ref name=":13" /> They can apply certain emotion regulation tactics to influence various emotional states.<ref name=":14" /> Additionally, maturation of brain functioning and language and motor skills permits toddlers to manage their emotional responses and levels of arousal more effectively.<ref>Rueda, M. R., Posner, M. I., & Rothbart, M. K. (2004). Attentional control and self-regulation. In R. F. Baumeister & K. D. Vohs (Eds.),'' Handbook of self-regulation: Research, theory, and applications ''(pp. 283–300). New York: Guilford Press.</ref>

Extrinsic emotion regulation remains important to emotional development in toddlerhood. Toddlers can learn ways from their caregivers to control their emotions and behaviors.<ref name="Kopp"/> For example, caregivers help teach self-regulation methods by distracting children from unpleasant events (like a [[vaccination]] shot) or helping them understand frightening events.<ref name="Thompson, 1994"/>

===Childhood===
Emotion regulation knowledge becomes more substantial during childhood. For example, children aged six to ten begin to understand [[display rules]]. They come to appreciate the contexts in which certain emotional expressions are socially most appropriate and therefore ought to be regulated. For example, children may understand that upon receiving a gift they should display a smile, irrespective of their actual feelings about the gift.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Harris | first1 = P. L. | year = 1983 | title = Children's understanding of the link between situation and emotion | journal = Journal of Experimental Child Psychology | volume = 33 | issue = 3| pages = 1–20 | doi=10.1016/0022-0965(83)90048-6}}</ref> During childhood, there is also a trend towards the use of more cognitive emotion regulation strategies, taking the place of more basic distraction, approach, and avoidance tactics.<ref>Stegge, H. & Terwog, M. M. (2007). Awareness and regulation of emotion in typical and atypical development. In J. J. Gross (Ed.), ''Handbook of Emotion Regulation'' (pp. 269–286). New York: Guilford Press.</ref>

Regarding the development of emotion dysregulation in children, one robust finding suggests that children who are frequently exposed to negative emotion at home will be more likely to display, and have difficulties regulating, high levels of negative emotion.<ref name="Caspi et. al, 2004">{{cite journal|last1=Caspi|first1=A.|author2=Moffitt, T.E. |author3=Morgan, J. |author4=Rutter, M. |author5=Taylor, A. |author6=Arseneault, L. |author7=Tully, L. |author8=Jacobs, C. |author9=Kim-Cohen, J. |author10=Polo-Tomas, M. |title=Maternal expressed emotion predicts children's antisocial behaviour problems: Using monozygotic-twin differences to identify environmental effects on behavioural development|journal=Developmental Psychology|year=2004|volume=40|pages=149–161|doi=10.1037/0012-1649.40.2.149|pmid=14979757|issue=2 |hdl=10818/12931 }}</ref><ref name="Eisenberg, Zhou & Koller, 2001">{{cite journal|last1=Eisenberg|first1=N.|author2=Zhou, Q. |author3=Koller, S. |title=Brazilian adolescents' prosocial moral judgement and behaviour: Relations to sympathy, perspective-taking, gender-role orientation, and demographic characteristics|journal=Child Development|year=2001|volume=72|pages=518–534|doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00294|pmid=11333082|issue=2}}</ref><ref name="Maughan & Cicchetti">{{cite journal|last1=Maughan|first1=A.|author2=Cicchetti, D.|title=Impact of child maltreatment and interadult violence on children's emotional regulation abilities and socioemotional adjustment|journal=Child Development|year=2002|volume=73|pages=1525–1542|doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00488|pmid=12361317|issue=5}}</ref><ref name="Valiente, Eisenberg & Spinrad, 2004">{{cite journal|last1=Valiente|first1=C.|author2=Fabes, R.A. |author3=Eisenberg, N. |author4=Spinrad, T.L. |title=The relations of parental expressivity and support to children's coping with daily stress|journal=Journal of Family Psychology|year=2004|volume=18|pages=97–106|doi=10.1037/0893-3200.18.1.97|pmid=14992613|issue=1}}</ref>

===Adolescence===
[[Adolescence|Adolescents]] show a marked increase in their capacities to regulate their emotions, and emotion regulation decision making becomes more complex, depending on multiple factors. In particular, the significance of interpersonal outcomes increases for adolescents. When regulating their emotions, adolescents are therefore likely to take into account their social context.<ref name="ReferenceA"/> For instance, adolescents show a tendency to display more emotion if they expect a sympathetic response from their peers.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Zeman | first1 = J. | last2 = Garber | first2 = J. | year = 1996 | title = Display rules for anger, sadness, and pain: it depends on who is watching | journal = Child Development | volume = 67 | issue = 3| pages = 957–973 | doi=10.2307/1131873| jstor = 1131873 | pmid = 8706538 }}</ref>

Additionally, spontaneous use of cognitive emotion regulation strategies increases during adolescence, which is evidenced both by self-report data<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Garnefski | first1 = N. | last2 = Kraaij | first2 = V. | year = 2006 | title = Relationships between cognitive emotion regulation strategies and depressive symptoms: A comparative study of five specific samples | journal = Personality and Individual Differences | volume = 40 | issue = 8| pages = 1659–1669 | doi = 10.1016/j.paid.2005.12.009 }}</ref> and neural markers.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Luna | first1 = B. | last2 = Padmanabhan | first2 = A. | last3 = O'Hearn | first3 = K. | year = 2010 | title = What has fMRI told us about the development of cognitive control through adolescence? | journal = Brain and Cognition | volume = 72 | issue = 1| pages = 101–113 | doi = 10.1016/j.bandc.2009.08.005 | pmid = 19765880 | pmc = 2815087 }}</ref>

===Adulthood===
Social losses increase and health tends to decrease as people age. As people get older their motivation to seek emotional meaning in life through social ties tends to increase.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Carstensen | first1 = L. A. L. | last2 = Fung | first2 = H. | last3 = Charlse | first3 = S. | year = 2003 | title = Socioemotional selectivity theory and the regulation of emotion in the second half of life | journal = Motivation and Emotion | volume = 27 | issue = 2| pages = 103–123 | doi=10.1023/a:1024569803230| s2cid = 143149171 }}</ref> [[Autonomic nervous system|Autonomic]] responsiveness decreases with age, and emotion regulation skill tends to increase.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lawton | first1 = M. P. | year = 2001 | title = Emotion in later life | journal = Current Directions in Psychological Science | volume = 20 | issue = 4| pages = 120–123 | doi=10.1111/1467-8721.00130| s2cid = 146528874 }}</ref>

Emotional regulation in adulthood can also be examined in terms of positive and negative affectivity.<ref name=":11">{{Cite journal|last1=Ashby|first1=F. Gregory|last2=Isen|first2=Alice M.|last3=Turken|first3=And U.|date=1999|title=A neuropsychological theory of positive affect and its influence on cognition.|url=http://doi.apa.org/getdoi.cfm?doi=10.1037/0033-295X.106.3.529|journal=Psychological Review|language=en|volume=106|issue=3|pages=529–550|doi=10.1037/0033-295X.106.3.529|pmid=10467897|issn=1939-1471|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Positive and negative affectivity refers to the types of emotions felt by an individual as well as the way those emotions are expressed.<ref name=":11" /> With adulthood comes an increased ability to maintain both high positive affectivity and low negative affectivity "more rapidly than adolescents."<ref name=":12">{{Cite journal|last1=O'Rourke|first1=Norm|last2=Cappeliez|first2=Philippe|last3=Claxton|first3=Amy|date=2011-03-01|title=Functions of reminiscence and the psychological well-being of young-old and older adults over time|journal=Aging & Mental Health|volume=15|issue=2|pages=272–281|doi=10.1080/13607861003713281|issn=1360-7863|pmid=21140308|s2cid=34423670}}</ref> This response to life's challenges seems to become "automatized" as people progress throughout adulthood.<ref name=":12" /> Thus, as individuals age, their capability of self-regulating emotions and responding to their emotions in healthy ways improves.<ref name=":12" />

Additionally, emotional regulation may vary between young adults and older adults. Younger adults have been found to be more successful than older adults in practicing "cognitive reappraisal" to decrease negative internal emotions.<ref name=":15">{{Cite journal|last1=Urry|first1=Heather L.|last2=Gross|first2=James J.|date=2010-12-01|title=Emotion Regulation in Older Age|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|language=en|volume=19|issue=6|pages=352–357|doi=10.1177/0963721410388395|s2cid=1400335|issn=0963-7214}}</ref> On the other hand, older adults have been found to be more successful in the following emotional regulation areas:<ref name=":15" />
* Predicting the level of "emotional arousal" in possible situations
* Having a higher focus on positive information rather than negative
* Maintaining healthy levels of "hedonic well-being" (subjective well-being based on increased pleasure and decreased pain)

==Overview of perspectives==

===Neuropsychological perspective===

====Affective====
As people age, their [[affect (psychology)|affect]]{{spaced ndash}}the way they react to emotions{{spaced ndash}}changes, either positively or negatively. Studies show that positive affect increases as a person grows from adolescence to their mid 70s. Negative affect, on the other hand, decreases until the mid 70s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mroczek |first=Daniel K. |date=June 2001 |title=Age and Emotion in Adulthood |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/1467-8721.00122 |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |language=en |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=87–90 |doi=10.1111/1467-8721.00122 |issn=0963-7214|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Studies also show that emotions differ in adulthood, particularly affect (positive or negative).<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Magai |first1=Carol |last2=Consedine |first2=Nathan S. |last3=Krivoshekova |first3=Yulia S. |last4=Kudadjie-Gyamfi |first4=Elizabeth |last5=McPherson |first5=Renee |date=June 2006 |title=Emotion experience and expression across the adult life span: Insights from a multimodal assessment study. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0882-7974.21.2.303 |journal=Psychology and Aging |language=en |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=303–317 |doi=10.1037/0882-7974.21.2.303 |pmid=16768577 |issn=1939-1498|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Although some studies found that individuals experience less affect as they grow older, other studies have concluded that adults in their middle age experience more positive affect and less negative affect than younger adults. Positive affect was also higher for men than women while the negative affect was higher for women than it was for men and also for single people.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Steptoe |first1=Andrew |last2=Leigh |first2=Elizabeth S. |last3=Kumari |first3=Meena |date=December 2011 |title=Positive affect and distressed affect over the day in older people. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/a0023303 |journal=Psychology and Aging |language=en |volume=26 |issue=4 |pages=956–965 |doi=10.1037/a0023303 |pmid=21517182 |issn=1939-1498|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

A reason that older people{{spaced ndash}}middle adulthood{{spaced ndash}}might have less negative affect is because they have overcome, "the trials and vicissitudes of youth, they may increasingly experience a more pleasant balance of affect, at least up until their mid-70s". Positive affect might rise during middle age but towards the later years of life{{spaced ndash}}the 70s{{spaced ndash}}it begins to decline while negative affect also does the same. This might be due to failing health, reaching the end of their lives and the death of friends and relatives.<ref name="Labouvie">{{cite journal|last1=Labouvie-Vief|first1=Gisela|title=Dynamic Integration:Affect, Cognition and the Self in Adulthood|journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science|date=December 2003|volume=12|issue=6|pages=201–206|doi=10.1046/j.0963-7214.2003.01262.x|s2cid=145494340}}</ref>

In addition to [[Reference range|baseline]] levels of positive and negative affect, studies have found individual differences in the time-course of emotional responses to [[stimulus (psychology)|stimuli]]. The [[Time|temporal]] dynamics of emotion regulation, also known as affective [[chronometry]], include two key variables in the emotional response process: rise time to peak emotional response, and recovery time to baseline levels of emotion.<ref name="Davidson, R. J. 1998 pp. 307-330">{{cite journal|author=Davidson, R. J. |title= Affective Style and Affective Disorders: Perspectives from Affective Neuroscience|journal= Cognition and Emotion|volume= 12|year=1998|pages=307–330|doi=10.1080/026999398379628|issue=3|citeseerx= 10.1.1.670.1877}}</ref> Studies of affective chronometry typically separate positive and negative affect into distinct categories, as previous research has shown (despite some correlation) the ability of humans to experience changes in these categories independently of one another.<ref>Ruef, Anna Marie; Levenson, Robert W. Coan, James A. (Ed); Allen, John J. B. (Ed) "Continuous measurement of emotion: The affect rating dial" (2007). Handbook of emotion elicitation and assessment. Series in affective science., New York, NY, US: Oxford University Press, pp. 287–297.</ref> Affective chronometry research has been conducted on clinical populations with [[anxiety]], [[Mood (psychology)|mood]], and [[personality disorders]], but is also utilized as a measurement to test the effectiveness of different therapeutic techniques (including [[Mindfulness (psychology)|mindfulness]] training) on [[emotional dysregulation]].<ref>{{cite journal|author=Geschwind N.|pmid=21767001|year=2011|last2=Peeters|first2=F|last3=Drukker|first3=M|last4=Van Os|first4=J|last5=Wichers|first5=M|s2cid=3795249|title=Mindfulness training increases momentary positive emotions and reward experience in adults vulnerable to depression: A randomized controlled trial|volume=79|issue=5|pages=618–28|doi=10.1037/a0024595|journal=Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology|url=https://lirias.kuleuven.be/handle/123456789/332894}}</ref>

====Neurological====
The development of functional [[magnetic resonance imaging|magnetic resonance]] imaging has allowed for the study of emotion regulation on a biological level. Specifically, research over the last decade strongly suggests that there is a neural basis.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Frank | first1 = DW | last2 = Dewitt | first2 = M | last3 = Hudgens-Haney | first3 = ME | last4 = Schaeffer | first4 = DJ | last5 = Ball | first5 = BH | last6 = Schwarz | first6 = NF | last7 = Hussein | first7 = AA | last8 = Smart | first8 = LM | last9 = Sabatinelli | first9 = D | year = 2014 | title = Emotion regulation: Quantitative meta-analysis of functional activation and deactivation | journal = Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews | volume = 45 | pages = 202–211 | doi=10.1016/j.neubiorev.2014.06.010| pmid = 24984244 | s2cid = 22933500 }}</ref> Sufficient evidence has correlated emotion regulation to particular patterns of prefrontal activation. These regions include the [[orbit (anatomy)|orbit]]al prefrontal cortex, the ventromedial [[prefrontal cortex]], and the [[dorsolateral]] prefrontal cortex. Two additional brain structures that have been found to contribute are the amygdala and the anterior cingulate cortex.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=de Joode |first1=Niels T. |last2=Thorsen |first2=Anders L. |last3=Vester |first3=Eline L. |last4=Vriend |first4=Chris |last5=Pouwels |first5=Petra J.W. |last6=Hagen |first6=Kristen |last7=Ousdal |first7=Olga T. |last8=Hansen |first8=Bjarne |last9=Kvale |first9=Gerd |last10=van den Heuvel |first10=Odile A. |date=2022 |title=Longitudinal changes in neurometabolite concentrations in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex after concentrated exposure therapy for obsessive-compulsive disorder |journal=Journal of Affective Disorders |language=en |volume=299 |pages=344–352 |doi=10.1016/j.jad.2021.12.014|pmid=34920037 |s2cid=245266740 |doi-access=free |hdl=11250/2982832 |hdl-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Herwig |first1=U. |last2=Lutz |first2=J. |last3=Scherpiet |first3=S. |last4=Scheerer |first4=H. |last5=Kohlberg |first5=J. |last6=Opialla |first6=S. |last7=Preuss |first7=A. |last8=Steiger |first8=V.R. |last9=Sulzer |first9=J. |last10=Weidt |first10=S. |last11=Stämpfli |first11=P. |last12=Rufer |first12=M. |last13=Seifritz |first13=E. |last14=Jäncke |first14=L. |last15=Brühl |first15=A.B. |date=2019 |title=Training emotion regulation through real-time fMRI neurofeedback of amygdala activity |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1053811918319402 |journal=NeuroImage |language=en |volume=184 |pages=687–696 |doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2018.09.068|pmid=30287300 |s2cid=52918820 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Each of these structures are involved in various facets of emotion regulation and irregularities in one or more regions and/or interconnections among them are affiliated with failures of emotion regulation. An implication to these findings is that individual differences in prefrontal activation predict the ability to perform various tasks in aspects of emotion regulation.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal | last1 = Davidson | first1 = R.J. | last2 = Putnam | first2 = K.M. | last3 = Larson | first3 = C.L. | s2cid = 9857157 | year = 2000 | title = Dysfunction in the neural circuitry of emotion regulation: A possible prelude to violence | journal = Science | volume = 289 | issue = 5479| pages = 591–594 | doi=10.1126/science.289.5479.591| pmid = 10915615 | bibcode = 2000Sci...289..591D }}</ref> As for the peripheral nervous system, studies showed that while some emotional markers, like electrodermal activity and facial electromyography, decrease during emotion regulation,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Shiota |first1=Michelle N. |last2=Levenson |first2=Robert W. |date=December 2009 |title=Effects of aging on experimentally instructed detached reappraisal, positive reappraisal, and emotional behavior suppression |journal=Psychology and Aging |volume=24 |issue=4 |pages=890–900 |doi=10.1037/a0017896 |issn=1939-1498 |pmc=2805117 |pmid=20025404}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Labuschagne |first1=Izelle |last2=Pedder |first2=David J. |last3=Henry |first3=Julie D. |last4=Terrett |first4=Gill |last5=Rendell |first5=Peter G. |date=2020 |title=Age Differences in Emotion Regulation and Facial Muscle Reactivity to Emotional Films |url=https://karger.com/article/doi/10.1159/000501584 |journal=Gerontology |language=en |volume=66 |issue=1 |pages=74–84 |doi=10.1159/000501584 |pmid=31390633 |issn=0304-324X}}</ref> cardiac activity seem to get higher,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mohammed |first1=Abdul-Raheem |last2=Kosonogov |first2=Vladimir |last3=Lyusin |first3=Dmitry |date=September 2021 |title=Expressive suppression versus cognitive reappraisal: Effects on self-report and peripheral psychophysiology |journal=International Journal of Psychophysiology |volume=167 |pages=30–37 |doi=10.1016/j.ijpsycho.2021.06.007 |pmid=34157337 |issn=0167-8760}}</ref> reflecting heart efforts allocated to control emotions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=De Pascalis |first1=V. |last2=Barry |first2=R.J. |last3=Sparita |first3=A. |date=June 1995 |title=Decelerative changes in heart rate during recognition of visual stimuli: effects of psychological stress |journal=International Journal of Psychophysiology |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=21–31 |doi=10.1016/0167-8760(95)00023-l |pmid=8543481 |issn=0167-8760}}</ref>

===Sociological===
People intuitively mimic [[facial expressions]]; it is a fundamental part of healthy functioning. Similarities across cultures in regards to [[nonverbal communication]] has prompted the debate that it is in fact a [[universal language]].<ref name="Elfenbein, H. A. 2002">{{cite journal | last1 = Elfenbein | first1 = H. A. | last2 = Ambady | first2 = N. | s2cid = 16073381 | year = 2002 | title = On the universality and cultural specificity of emotion recognition: a meta-analysis | journal = Psychological Bulletin | volume = 128 | issue = 2| pages = 203–235 | doi=10.1037/0033-2909.128.2.203 | pmid=11931516}}</ref> It can be argued that emotion regulation plays a key role in the ability to generate the correct responses in social situations. Humans have control over facial expressions both [[conscious mind|consciously]] and [[unconscious mind|unconsciously]]: an intrinsic emotion program is generated as the result of a transaction with the world, which immediately results in an emotional response and usually a facial reaction.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Ekman | first1 = P. | last2 = Friesen | first2 = W. V. | last3 = Ancoli | first3 = S. | year = 1980 | title = Facial signs of emotional experience | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 39 | issue = 6| pages = 1125–1134 | doi=10.1037/h0077722| citeseerx = 10.1.1.306.6112 | s2cid = 14801813 }}</ref> It is a well documented [[phenomenon]] that emotions have an effect on facial expression, but recent research has provided evidence that the opposite may also be true.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Izard | first1 = C. E. | s2cid = 2627544 | year = 1990 | title = Facial expressions and the regulation of emotions | journal = Journal of Personality and Social Psychology | volume = 58 | issue = 3| pages = 487–498 | pmid = 2182826 | doi=10.1037/0022-3514.58.3.487}}</ref>

This notion would give rise to the belief that a person may not only control his emotion but in fact influence them as well. Emotion regulation focuses on providing the appropriate emotion in the appropriate circumstances. Some theories allude to the thought that each emotion serves a specific purpose in coordinating [[organism]]ic needs with [[Environmental psychology|environmental]] demands (Cole, 1994). This skill, although apparent throughout all nationalities,<ref name="Elfenbein, H. A. 2002"/> has been shown to vary in successful application at different age groups. In experiments done comparing younger and older adults to the same unpleasant stimuli, older adults were able to regulate their emotional reactions in a way that seemed to avoid negative confrontation.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Charles | first1 = S. T. | last2 = Carstensen | first2 = L. L. | year = 2008 | title = Unpleasant situations elicit different emotional responses in younger and older adults | journal = Psychology and Aging | volume = 23 | issue = 3| pages = 495–504 | doi = 10.1037/a0013284 | pmid = 18808240 | pmc = 2677442 }}</ref> These findings support the theory that with time people develop a better ability to regulate their emotions. This ability found in adults seems to better allow individuals to react in what would be considered a more appropriate manner in some social situations, permitting them to avoid adverse situations that could be seen as detrimental.

====Expressive regulation (in solitary conditions)====
In solitary conditions, emotion regulation can include a minimization-miniaturization effect, in which common outward expressive patterns are replaced with toned down versions of expression.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Holodynski |first=Manfred |date=2004 |title=The Miniaturization of Expression in the Development of Emotional Self-Regulation. |url=https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/0012-1649.40.1.16 |journal=Developmental Psychology |language=en |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=16–28 |doi=10.1037/0012-1649.40.1.16 |pmid=14700461 |issn=1939-0599|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Unlike other situations, in which physical expression (and its regulation) serve a social purpose (i.e. conforming to [[display rules]] or revealing emotion to outsiders), solitary conditions require no reason for emotions to be outwardly expressed (although intense levels of emotion can bring out noticeable expression anyway). The idea behind this is that as people get older, they learn that the purpose of outward expression (to appeal to other people), is not necessary in situations in which there is no one to appeal to.<ref name="Holodynski">{{cite journal|last1=Holodynski|first1=Manfred|title=The Miniaturization of Expression in the Development of Self-Regulation of Emotion|journal=Developmental Psychology|year=2004|volume=40|issue=1|pages=16–28|doi=10.1037/0012-1649.40.1.16|pmid=14700461}}</ref> As a result, the level of emotional expression can be lower in these solitary situations.

===Stress===
The way an individual reacts to stress can directly overlap with their ability to regulate emotion.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Gruhn |first1=Meredith A. |last2=Compas |first2=Bruce E. |date=2020 |title=Effects of maltreatment on coping and emotion regulation in childhood and adolescence: A meta-analytic review |journal=Child Abuse & Neglect |language=en |volume=103 |article-number=104446 |doi=10.1016/j.chiabu.2020.104446|pmid=32200195 |pmc=12352122 |s2cid=214616457 }}</ref> Although the two concepts differ in a multitude of ways, "both coping [with stress] and emotion regulation involve affect modulation and appraisal processes" that are necessary for healthy relationships and self-identity.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Wang|first1=Manjie|last2=Saudino|first2=Kimberly J.|date=2011-01-11|title=Emotion Regulation and Stress|journal=Journal of Adult Development|volume=18|issue=2|pages=95–103|doi=10.1007/s10804-010-9114-7|s2cid=144146368|issn=1068-0667}}</ref>

According to Yu. V. Shcherbatykh, emotional stress in situations like school examinations can be reduced by engaging in self-regulating activities prior to the task being performed. To study the influence of [[Self-control|self-regulation]] on mental and [[physiological]] processes under exam stress, Shcherbatykh conducted a test with an experimental group of 28 students (of both sexes) and a control group of 102 students (also of both sexes).<ref name="Shcherbatykh">{{cite journal|last1=Shcherbatykh|first1=Yu. V.|title=Self-Regulation of Autonomic Homeostasis in Emotional Stress|journal=Human Physiology|year=2000|volume=26|issue=5|pages=641–642|url=https://www.no-stress.ru/stress/stress_science/emotional_stress_regulation.html|doi=10.1007/BF02760382|pmid=11059163|s2cid=36985305|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

In the moments before the examination, [[situational]] stress levels were raised in both groups from what they were in quiet states. In the experimental group, participants engaged in three self-regulating techniques (concentration on respiration, general body relaxation, and the creation of a mental image of successfully passing the examination). During the examination, the anxiety levels of the experimental group were lower than that of the control group. Also, the percent of unsatisfactory marks in the experimental group was 1.7 times less than in the control group. From this data, Shcherbatykh concluded that the application of self-regulating actions before examinations helps to significantly reduce levels of emotional strain, which can help lead to better performance results.<ref name="Shcherbatykh" />

Emotion regulation has also been associated with physiological responses to stress during laboratory stress paradigms.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Griffin|first1=Siobhán M.|last2=Howard|first2=Siobhán|date=2022|title=Individual differences in emotion regulation and cardiovascular responding to stress|url=https://psycnet.apa.org/doiLanding?doi=10.1037%2Femo0001037|journal=Emotion|volume=22|issue=2|pages=331–345|doi=10.1037/emo0001037|pmid=34807696 |s2cid=244491171 }}</ref>

===Decision making===
Identification of our emotional self-regulating process can facilitate in the decision-making process.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Miclea, M. |author2=Miu, A. |s2cid=18194230 |pmid=20364902|title=Emotion Regulation and Decision Making Under Risk and Uncertainty|year=2010|volume=10|issue=2|pages=257–65|doi=10.1037/a0018489|journal=Emotion}}</ref> Current literature on emotion regulation identifies that humans characteristically make efforts in controlling emotion experiences.<ref>{{cite journal|author=Gross, J. J.|pmid=12212647|year=2002|title=Emotion regulation: Affective, cognitive, and social consequences|volume=39|issue=3|pages=281–91|journal=Psychophysiology|doi=10.1017/s0048577201393198|doi-access=free}}</ref> There is then a possibility that our present state emotions can be altered by emotion regulation strategies resulting in the possibility that different regulation strategies could have different decision implications.

===Digital emotion regulation===
Following widespread adoption in the 21st century of digital devices and services for use in everyday life, evidence is mounting that people are increasingly using these tools to manage and regulate moods and emotions.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Wadley, G. |author2=Smith, W. |author3=Koval, P. |author4=Gross, J. |title=Digital Emotion Regulation |date=2020 |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |volume=29 |issue=4 |pages=412–418 |doi=10.1177/0963721420920592|s2cid=215412759 |doi-access=free |hdl=11343/235572 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> A wide range of digital resources are used for emotion regulation including emotional AI,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Zhang |first=Heng |last2=Liu |first2=Yuhan |last3=Jiang |first3=Meilin |last4=Chen |first4=Juanjuan |last5=Wang |first5=Minhong |last6=Paas |first6=Fred |date=2025-11-15 |title=Emotional Artificial Intelligence in Education: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |url=https://doi.org/10.1007/s10648-025-10086-4 |journal=Educational Psychology Review |language=en |volume=37 |issue=4 |pages=106 |doi=10.1007/s10648-025-10086-4 |issn=1573-336X}}</ref> smartphones,<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Rozgonjuk, D. |author2=Elhai, J. |title=Emotion regulation in relation to smartphone use: Process smartphone use mediates the association between expressive suppression and problematic smartphone use.
