|journal=Current Psychology |date=2021 |volume=40 |issue=7 |pages=3246–3255 |doi=10.1007/s12144-019-00271-4|s2cid=150551972 }}</ref> social media,<ref>{{cite book |author1=Blumberg, F. |author2=Rice, J. |author3=Dickmeis, A. |date=2016 |editor-last=Tettegah |editor-first=Sharon |title=Emotions, Technology, and Social Media |publisher=Elsevier, Amsterdam |pages=105–116 |chapter=Social media as a venue for emotion regulation among adolescents }}</ref> streaming services,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Myrick |first1=Jessica |title=Emotion regulation, procrastination, and watching cat videos online: Who watches Internet cats, why, and to what effect? |journal=Computers in Human Behavior |date=2015 |volume=52 |pages=168–176 |doi=10.1016/j.chb.2015.06.001|s2cid=16187524 }}</ref> online shopping,<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Bui, M. |author2=Kemp, E. |title=E-tail emotion regulation: examining online hedonic product purchases |journal=International Journal of Retail & Distribution Management |date=2013 |volume=41 |issue=2 |pages=155–170 |doi=10.1108/09590551311304338|s2cid=143290039 }}</ref> and videogames.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Villani, D. |author2=Carissoli, C. |author3=Triberti, S. |author4=Marchetti, A. |author5=Gilli, G. |author6=Riva, G. |title=Videogames for Emotion Regulation: A Systematic Review |journal=Games for Health Journal |date=2018 |volume=7 |issue=2 |pages=85–99 |doi=10.1089/g4h.2017.0108|pmid=29424555 |s2cid=4701955 |doi-access=free |hdl=2434/696407 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> Such spontaneous forms of digital emotion regulation can be distinguished from the use of digital interventions such as smartphone apps that have been explicitly designed to support emotional regulation or teach emotion regulation skills in clinical and non-clinical populations.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Slovak, P. |author2=Antle, A. |author3=Theofanopoulou, N. |author4=Roquet, C. |author5=Gross, J. |author6=Isbister, K. |title=Designing for emotion regulation interventions: an agenda for HCI theory and research |journal=ACM Transactions on Computer-Human Interaction |date=2022 |volume=30 |pages=1–51 |doi=10.1145/3569898|s2cid=247922819 |doi-access=free |arxiv=2204.00118 }}</ref> Digital implementation of emotion regulation strategies can occur at all stages of the process model and in all strategy families, including interpersonal emotion regulation.<ref>{{cite book |author1=Smith, W. |author2=Wadley, G. |author3=Webber, S. |author4=Tag, B. |author5=Kostakos, V. |author6=Koval, P. |author7=Gross, J. |title=CHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems |chapter=Digital Emotion Regulation in Everyday Life |date=2022 |pages=1–15 |doi=10.1145/3491102.3517573|isbn=978-1-4503-9157-3 |s2cid=248419517 }}</ref>

==Effects of low self-regulation==
{{More citations needed section|date=January 2026}}
Issues with emotional regulation, a common consequence of [[Adverse childhood experiences|Adverse Childhood Experiences]] (ACEs), have been shown to be a strong factor linking ACEs with psychological and physical dysfunctions in adulthood such as PTSD or depression.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Cloitre |first1=Marylene |last2=Khan |first2=Christina |last3=Mackintosh |first3=Margaret-Anne |last4=Garvert |first4=Donn W. |last5=Henn-Haase |first5=Clare M. |last6=Falvey |first6=Erin C. |last7=Saito |first7=Jean |date=January 2019 |title=Emotion regulation mediates the relationship between ACES and physical and mental health. |journal=Psychological Trauma: Theory, Research, Practice, and Policy |language=en |volume=11 |issue=1 |pages=82–89 |doi=10.1037/tra0000374 |issn=1942-969X |pmid=29745688 |s2cid=13664483 |doi-access=free}}</ref> 

A Ningxia University study on emotional regulation self-efficacy and impulsivity effects on risk-taking behaviour published in ''Frontiers in Psychology'' in 2025 defines emotional regulation self-efficacy as "an individual's confidence in their ability to regulate their own emotions, which primarily includes self-efficacy in regulating negative emotions and self-efficacy in regulating positive emotions."<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Ruoyu |last2=Zhang |first2=Chen |last3=Huang |first3=Liying |date=2025-06-18 |title=Emotional regulation self-efficacy and impulsivity effects on college students' risk-taking behavior: a cross-sectional study |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |language=English |volume=16 |article-number=1566618 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2025.1566618 |doi-access=free |issn=1664-1078 |pmc=12213758 |pmid=40606903}}</ref> 

High emotional regulation self-efficacy is associated with stronger domain-specific efficacy beliefs which in turn directly predict outcomes such as [[Prosocial behavior|prosocial behaviour]], delinquency and depression. Examples of domain-specific efficacy beliefs are academic self-efficacy (the ability to manage school work), resistive self-regulatory self-efficacy (resisting peer pressure towards anti-social acts) and empathic self-efficacy (engaging with others' feelings). This seems to suggest that emotional regulation self-efficacy enables effective self-regulation in other domains and thus, allows for more positive behaviours and outcomes as a downstream effect.<ref>{{cite journal |author1=Bandura, A. |author2=Caprara, G. V. |author3=Barbaranelli, C. |author4=Gerbino, M. |author5=Pastorelli, C. |year=2003 |title=Role of Affective Self-Regulatory Efficacy in Diverse Spheres of Psychosocial Functioning |journal=Child Development |volume=74 |issue=3 |pages=769–82 |doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00567 |jstor=3696228 |pmid=12795389 |s2cid=6671293}}</ref>

These traumatic experiences typically occur during grade school {{Opinion|date=January 2026}} and are sometimes associated with [[bullying]].{{Citation needed|date=January 2026|reason=Both claims not cited}} Children who can't properly self-regulate express their volatile emotions in a variety of ways, including screaming if they don't have their way, lashing out with their fists, throwing objects (such as chairs), or bullying other children.{{Citation needed|date=January 2026|reason=Evidence that this is how it manifests}} These behaviours may elicit negative reactions from the social environment, which, in turn, can exacerbate or maintain the original regulation problems over time, a process termed cumulative continuity. {{Citation needed|date=January 2026|reason=Evidence that this phenomenon exists}} These children are more likely to have conflict-based relationships with their teachers and other children. Early behaviour problems and negative teacher-child relationships in kindergarten predict more behavioural and academic difficulties through adolescence.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hamre |first1=Bridget K. |last2=Pianta |first2=Robert C. |date=March–April 2001 |title=Early Teacher–Child Relationships and the Trajectory of Children's School Outcomes through Eighth Grade |url=https://srcd.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1467-8624.00301 |journal=Child Development |language=en |volume=72 |issue=2 |pages=625–638 |doi=10.1111/1467-8624.00301 |pmid=11333089 |issn=0009-3920|url-access=subscription }}</ref> 

As most peers do not have low self-regulation, the children struggling may seem more conspicuous, and become excluded from social groups, teased or harassed by their peers. This atypical behaviour may result in teenagers becoming social outcasts in their respective social groups, causing them to lash out in angry and potentially violent ways. {{Citation needed|date=January 2026|reason=Claim that they are singled out, and that this may lead to lashing out both need citations}} Childhood teasing, bullying or social exclusion can be especially damaging as it could lead to psychological symptoms such as depression and anxiety (in which dysregulated emotions play a central role), which, in turn, could lead to more peer victimization.<ref>{{cite journal|author1=Reijntjesa, A. |author2=Kamphuisb, J. H. |author3=Prinziea, P. |author4=Telchc, M.J. |title=Peer victimization and internalizing problems in children: A meta-analysis of longitudinal studies|pmid=20304490 |year=2010|volume=34|issue=4|pages=244–52|journal=Child Abuse & Neglect|doi=10.1016/j.chiabu.2009.07.009}}</ref> This is why it is recommended to foster emotional self-regulation in children as early as possible. {{Opinion|date=January 2026}}

==Occupational therapy in schools==

[[Occupational therapist]]s (OTs) are integrated educators in most public and private schools across the United States. They are trained in mental health and activity analysis to assess the needs of their clients. OTs and students work together to create meaningful and healthy habits for stress management, social skills, emotional labeling, coping strategies, awareness, problem-solving, self-monitoring, judgment, emotional control, and others in the school and home environment.<ref name="theottoolbox.com">{{cite web | url=https://www.theottoolbox.com/emotional-regulation-and-executive-function/ | title=Emotional Regulation and Executive Function | date=8 July 2021 }}</ref><ref name="research.aota.org">{{Cite journal |last1=Pfirman |first1=Nicole |last2=Rivera |first2=Chelsea |last3=Saffer |first3=Abe |date=30 March 2022 |title=Health Promotion and Wellness for All Students: School-Based Occupational Therapy as a Preventive Approach |url=https://research.aota.org/ajot/article-abstract/77/2/7702090010/24067/Health-Promotion-and-Wellness-for-All-Students?redirectedFrom=fulltext |journal=The American Journal of Occupational Therapy |volume=77 |issue=2 |article-number=7702090010 |doi=10.5014/ajot.2023.050242|pmid=36996456 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> OTs can complete formal assessments for emotional regulation and treat in a client-centered manner for each student.<ref name="research.aota.org"/> In addition, they can create individualized home programs for carryover with their families. For example, OTs can work with students to engage in the occupational therapist-developed curriculum The Zones of Regulation,<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://jayscholar.etown.edu/otstu/7/ | title=The Use of the Zones of Regulation® in an Elementary School: Student and Teacher Perceptions | journal=Occupational Therapy: Student Scholarship & Creative Works | date=April 2016 | last1=Mutter | first1=Carly }}</ref> which utilizes evidence-based knowledge, formal assessment, and in-classroom treatment to improve self-regulation of emotional behaviors and create long-lasting changes in habits.{{Fact or opinion|date=January 2026}}

Early childhood access to education on emotional regulation mitigates risk factors for increased anxiety, depression, and negative behaviors. It allows the student to create healthy habits for school and home environments.<ref name="research.aota.org"/> Children should be able to learn to regulate their feelings for full participation in activities, including social skills, play, sports, and school.{{Opinion|date=January 2026}}
